title: Supreme Court Debates Birthright Citizenship
source: The Guardian - The Week USA – April 10, 2026
content:
In a remarkable break with centuries of tradition, the President personally attended Supreme Court arguments to listen as his administration asked the justices to overturn birthright citizenship. During the questioning, the Chief Justice and several other members of the court cast doubt on the constitutionality of a recent executive order. This controversial order requires proof of parental citizenship or permanent residency for a newborn to become a United States citizen. If upheld by the court, the executive order could effectively deny citizenship to roughly a quarter of a million babies every year. The administration's solicitor general argued that a modern trend of 'birth tourism' necessitates these strict new legal restrictions.

Despite the administration's arguments, the justices aggressively pushed back against the proposed changes to established constitutional law. The Chief Justice noted that while the modern world has changed, the text of the Constitution remains exactly the same. Furthermore, most of the justices appeared deeply skeptical, finding the administration's supporting examples to be highly unusual and quirky. However, one conservative justice suggested that an 1898 precedent regarding the U.S.-born child of immigrants might not be the final word on the Fourteenth Amendment. He argued that the court was now dealing with the widespread phenomenon of illegal immigration, a concept that was essentially unknown when the amendment was drafted.
